_meta: Qualitative chemical tests for JEE Advanced organic chemistry. Used by the generator to enrich questions and add scope/exception context. Each entry covers: reagents, positive result, scope_nodes (graph node IDs), exceptions, and JEE-specific traps.

tollens.name: Tollens' Test (Silver Mirror Test)
tollens.conditions: Warm gently with fresh Tollens' reagent in a clean glass tube; do not boil
tollens.positive_result: Silver mirror deposited on the inner wall of the test tube; silver precipitate
tollens.scope_note: All aldehydes (aliphatic and aromatic); reducing sugars (which contain free aldehyde group)

false_positives: Formic acid (HCOOH) gives silver mirror because it is also an aldehyde-type reducing agent (can be oxidised to CO₂)
Fructose gives positive (reducing sugar via enediol tautomerism in alkaline conditions)
Glucose, maltose, lactose all give positive (reducing sugars)
false_negatives: Ketones give NEGATIVE — cannot be oxidised by mild Tollens' reagent
Sucrose gives NEGATIVE — non-reducing sugar (no free anomeric OH)
exceptions.scope_restrictions: No restriction between aliphatic vs aromatic — benzaldehyde gives positive (slow)

tollens.mechanism_note: Aldehyde is oxidised to carboxylate; Ag⁺ is reduced to Ag metal (Ag⁰) which deposits as mirror
jee_traps: Trap 1: Benzaldehyde gives positive with Tollens' but NEGATIVE with Fehling's — the key distinction between the two tests
Trap 2: Fructose (a ketose) gives positive with Tollens' because in alkaline conditions it isomerises via enediol to an aldose form
Trap 3: Formic acid gives positive (unusual — it is the only carboxylic acid that reduces Tollens')
Trap 4: Acetaldehyde gives positive (aldehyde); acetone gives NEGATIVE (ketone) — Tollens' distinguishes them

fehlings.name: Fehling's Test
reagents: Fehling's solution B (NaOH + sodium potassium tartrate)
fehlings.conditions: Mix equal volumes of Fehling's A and B to get deep blue solution; add compound and heat
fehlings.positive_result: Brick-red (orange-red) precipitate of Cu₂O; blue colour of solution disappears
fehlings.scope_note: Aliphatic aldehydes only; reducing sugars. DOES NOT react with aromatic aldehydes.

false_positives: Reducing sugars (glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose) give positive
Fructose gives positive despite being a ketose (isomerises to aldose in alkaline conditions)
false_negatives: Benzaldehyde gives NEGATIVE — aromatic aldehydes do not reduce Fehling's (electron-poor ring stabilises the carbonyl against oxidation by Cu²⁺)
Ketones give NEGATIVE — cannot reduce Fehling's
Sucrose gives NEGATIVE (non-reducing sugar)
Starch gives NEGATIVE (non-reducing polysaccharide)
Formic acid — borderline; HCOOH can reduce Fehling's in some conditions
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Aliphatic aldehydes only — aromatic aldehydes (benzaldehyde, cinnamaldehyde) give NEGATIVE

fehlings.mechanism_note: Aliphatic aldehyde reduces Cu²⁺ (from CuSO₄ complexed with tartrate) to Cu⁺, which precipitates as Cu₂O brick-red solid
jee_traps: Trap 1: Benzaldehyde gives NEGATIVE with Fehling's but POSITIVE with Tollens' — this is the classic Fehling's vs Tollens' JEE distinction
Trap 2: Fructose (ketose) gives POSITIVE with Fehling's because alkaline conditions convert it to glucose-like aldose via enediol
Trap 3: Both Fehling's and Tollens' are positive for aliphatic aldehydes — use Fehling's specifically to detect aromatic vs aliphatic aldehyde
Trap 4: Sucrose is non-reducing — it gives NEGATIVE with both Fehling's and Tollens'

iodoform.name: Iodoform Test (Haloform Test with Iodine)
iodoform.conditions: Add I₂/NaOH at room temperature; warm gently if no immediate precipitate
iodoform.positive_result: Yellow crystalline precipitate of iodoform (CHI₃) with distinctive antiseptic odour
iodoform.scope_note: CH₃CO- group (methyl ketones, acetaldehyde) OR CH₃CH(OH)- group (secondary alcohols with methyl adjacent to CHOH, and ethanol)

false_positives: Ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) gives positive — the only primary alcohol that gives iodoform test
Isopropanol (propan-2-ol, CH₃CHOHCH₃) gives positive — secondary alcohol with CH₃CHOH- group
Acetaldehyde (ethanal, CH₃CHO) gives positive — the only aldehyde that gives iodoform (it has CH₃CO- group)
false_negatives: Methanol (CH₃OH) gives NEGATIVE — no CH₃CO- or CH₃CHOH- group
Formaldehyde (HCHO) gives NEGATIVE — has no methyl group
n-Propanol (propan-1-ol) gives NEGATIVE — CH₃CH₂CH₂OH has no CH₃CHOH- pattern
Benzaldehyde gives NEGATIVE
Diethyl ketone (pentan-3-one, CH₃CH₂COCH₂CH₃) gives NEGATIVE — no methyl adjacent to C=O
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Positive ONLY for: (1) methyl ketones R-CO-CH₃, (2) acetaldehyde CH₃CHO, (3) ethanol CH₃CH₂OH, (4) secondary alcohols with structure CH₃CHOH-R (e.g., isopropanol, butan-2-ol)

iodoform.mechanism_note: I₂/NaOH first oxidises CH₃CHOH- to CH₃CO-; then alpha-iodination of methyl group proceeds three times to give CI₃-CO-R; base then cleaves the CI₃-CO bond (haloform reaction) to give CHI₃ + RCOO⁻
jee_traps: Trap 1: Ethanol gives POSITIVE, methanol gives NEGATIVE — most commonly tested pair
Trap 2: Acetaldehyde gives POSITIVE, formaldehyde gives NEGATIVE
Trap 3: Isopropanol (2° alcohol) gives POSITIVE, n-propanol (1° primary) gives NEGATIVE
Trap 4: Butan-2-ol gives POSITIVE (CH₃CHOH-CH₂CH₃ has CH₃CHOH-), butan-1-ol gives NEGATIVE
Trap 5: The test identifies CH₃CO- group — NOT C=O in general; pentan-3-one (diethyl ketone) gives NEGATIVE

carbylamine.name: Carbylamine Test (Isocyanide Test)
carbylamine.conditions: Warm with CHCl₃ and excess alc. KOH; primary amine MUST be present
carbylamine.positive_result: Foul-smelling isocyanide (carbylamine) vapour — characteristically nauseating smell; no visible precipitate
carbylamine.scope_note: Primary amines ONLY (1°) — both aliphatic (R-NH₂) and aromatic (Ar-NH₂ including aniline)

false_negatives: Secondary amines (R₂NH) give NO reaction — no free NH₂ group to form isocyanide
Tertiary amines (R₃N) give NO reaction — no N-H bond available
Amides (R-CO-NH₂) give NEGATIVE — nitrogen lone pair is delocalised into carbonyl
Nitro compounds give NEGATIVE
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Exclusively primary amines (1°). The test FAILS for 2° and 3° amines — this is its primary analytical utility.

carbylamine.mechanism_note: CHCl₃ + KOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene); dichlorocarbene inserts into N-H of primary amine to give RNH-CHCl₂; two dehydrohalogenation steps give R-N≡C (isocyanide)
jee_traps: Trap 1: Secondary and tertiary amines give NEGATIVE — this is the key distinction; carbylamine distinguishes 1° from 2°/3°
Trap 2: Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) gives POSITIVE — it is a primary amine (aromatic, but still 1°)
Trap 3: The product isocyanide is NOT the same as isocyanate (R-NCO) — isocyanide is R-NC with no oxygen
Trap 4: Alcoholic KOH is essential, not aqueous KOH — aqueous KOH gives different products
Trap 5: Amides (1° amide R-CONH₂) look like primary amines but give NEGATIVE — amide nitrogen is deactivated

lucas.name: Lucas Test
lucas.conditions: Mix alcohol with Lucas reagent (ZnCl₂ + conc. HCl) at room temperature; observe turbidity
lucas.positive_result: Turbidity (cloudiness) due to formation of insoluble alkyl chloride (R-Cl layer separates)
lucas.scope_note: Alcohols — used to distinguish 1°, 2°, 3° by rate of turbidity formation

false_positives: Allylic alcohols (R-CH=CH-CH₂OH) behave like tertiary — immediate turbidity due to resonance-stabilised allylic carbocation
Benzylic alcohols (Ar-CH₂OH) behave like tertiary — immediate turbidity due to benzylic carbocation stability
false_negatives: Primary alcohols (except allylic/benzylic) give NO turbidity at room temperature — SN1 too slow for 1° carbocation
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Only applicable to low molecular weight (C1-C6) alcohols that are soluble in Lucas reagent (HCl/ZnCl₂). Higher alcohols are insoluble and give misleading results.

lucas.mechanism_note: ZnCl₂ coordinates to OH to make it a better leaving group; 3° and 2° alcohols undergo SN1 via stable carbocations to give R-Cl; 1° alcohols require heating for slow SN2
jee_traps: Trap 1: Tertiary alcohol → immediate turbidity; secondary → turbidity in ~5 min (may need gentle warming); primary → no turbidity at RT (requires heat/prolonged time)
Trap 2: Allylic and benzylic PRIMARY alcohols give immediate turbidity like 3° (stabilised cations) — don't assume turbidity = tertiary
Trap 3: Lucas test fails for alcohols with >6 carbons (insoluble in reagent)
Trap 4: 2-methylpropan-2-ol (tert-butanol) gives immediate turbidity; propan-1-ol gives no turbidity at RT

victor_meyer.name: Victor Meyer Test
victor_meyer.conditions: Multistep: (1) alcohol → alkyl iodide (P/I₂); (2) R-I → R-NO₂ (AgNO₂ or KNO₂); (3) R-NO₂ + HNO₂ → nitroso derivative; (4) dissolve in NaOH — observe colour
victor_meyer.positive_result: 1° alcohol → red colour; 2° alcohol → blue colour; 3° alcohol → colourless (no reaction in step 3)
victor_meyer.scope_note: Distinguishes primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols

false_negatives: 3° alcohols give colourless / no colour change with NaOH — they have no alpha-H on the nitroalkane to react with HNO₂
exceptions.scope_restrictions: The coloured product comes from the aci-form of 1° or 2° nitroalkanes reacting with NaOH. 3° nitroalkanes have no alpha-H so no aci-form → colourless.

victor_meyer.mechanism_note: Primary nitroalkane (R-CH₂-NO₂) has two alpha-H: forms red aci-form on treatment with NaOH; secondary nitroalkane (R₂CH-NO₂) has one alpha-H: forms blue aci-form; tertiary (R₃C-NO₂) has no alpha-H: no coloured product
jee_traps: Trap 1: 1° → red; 2° → blue; 3° → colourless — these exact colours are frequently tested
Trap 2: The coloured product is the aci-form (nitronic acid) of the nitroalkane, not the amine
Trap 3: This test identifies primary vs secondary vs tertiary ALCOHOLS (indirectly via nitroalkane chemistry)
Trap 4: Different from Lucas test — Victor Meyer uses nitrosation; Lucas uses HCl/ZnCl₂

hinsberg.name: Hinsberg Test
hinsberg.conditions: Shake amine with benzenesulfonyl chloride and aq. KOH; observe solubility
hinsberg.positive_result: 1° amine: soluble sulfonamide formed (acidic N-H → dissolves in KOH); 2° amine: insoluble sulfonamide precipitate; 3° amine: no sulfonamide (no reaction with sulfonyl chloride — N-H absent)
hinsberg.scope_note: Distinguishes primary, secondary, and tertiary amines

false_negatives: 3° amines give NO sulfonamide — they appear to 'not react' but are simply unreactive toward benzenesulfonyl chloride since there is no N-H
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Works for aliphatic and aromatic amines

hinsberg.mechanism_note: Benzenesulfonyl chloride reacts with N-H amines to form sulfonamide (C₆H₅SO₂-NR-); 1° sulfonamide has acidic N-H (pKa ~10) that dissolves in KOH; 2° sulfonamide has no N-H, so insoluble; 3° amine has no N-H to react
jee_traps: Trap 1: 3° amine gives NO product at all — not an insoluble product, just no sulfonamide
Trap 2: 1° amine sulfonamide is SOLUBLE in KOH (acidic N-H); 2° amine sulfonamide is INSOLUBLE (no N-H)
Trap 3: Aniline (1° aromatic amine) gives a soluble sulfonamide — do not assume 1° aromatic gives no reaction
Trap 4: This test distinguishes all three types; Lucas test only distinguishes alcohol degrees

baeyer.name: Baeyer's Test (Alkaline KMnO₄)
baeyer.conditions: Add cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) at room temperature; no heating
baeyer.positive_result: Purple colour of KMnO₄ is decolourised (KMnO₄ → MnO₂ brown precipitate); solution turns colourless or brown
baeyer.scope_note: Compounds with C=C, C≡C, or oxidisable groups (aldehydes). Used to detect unsaturation.

false_positives: Aldehydes decolourise cold KMnO₄ (oxidised to carboxylic acids) — so decolourisation does not exclusively mean unsaturation
Primary and secondary alcohols can slowly reduce KMnO₄ at room temperature
false_negatives: Benzene does NOT decolourise cold dilute KMnO₄ — aromatic stability prevents oxidation under mild conditions
Alkanes do NOT decolourise cold KMnO₄
Ketones do NOT decolourise cold KMnO₄ under mild conditions
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Cold and dilute conditions are critical — hot concentrated KMnO₄ oxidises toluene side chains and gives different products (not Baeyer's test)

baeyer.mechanism_note: KMnO₄ syn-dihydroxylates C=C (giving diol) or oxidises C≡C/CHO; Mn(VII) is reduced to Mn(IV) (MnO₂, brown) or Mn(II) (colourless), decolourising the purple reagent
jee_traps: Trap 1: Benzene does NOT decolourise cold KMnO₄ (aromatic) — toluene DOES with hot KMnO₄ (side-chain oxidation) — temperature matters
Trap 2: Baeyer's test detects unsaturation but aldehydes also give positive — specify 'Baeyer's test' ≠ 'test for alkene only'
Trap 3: Cyclohexene (alkene) gives positive; cyclohexane (alkane) gives negative — useful distinction
Trap 4: Acetylene (ethyne) gives positive with cold KMnO₄

can_test.name: CAN Test (Ceric Ammonium Nitrate Test)
can_test.conditions: Add a few drops of orange CAN reagent to compound; observe colour change
can_test.positive_result: Orange CAN reagent turns red (formation of red Ce-alcohol complex [Ce(NO₃)₄(ROH)₃])
can_test.scope_note: All alcohols (primary, secondary, tertiary) give red complex. Used to confirm presence of -OH group.

false_positives: Phenols give a different coloured complex (not consistently red — often different shades) — should not be confused with alcohol positive
false_negatives: Ethers give NEGATIVE — no -OH group, oxygen not accessible to Ce complex formation
Esters give NEGATIVE
Alkyl halides give NEGATIVE
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Tertiary alcohols also give positive (unlike Lucas test which is slow for 3° SN1 — CAN works for all three degrees equally)

can_test.mechanism_note: Cerium(IV) coordinates to the lone pair of the hydroxyl oxygen, forming a coloured Ce-alcohol complex
jee_traps: Trap 1: All three degrees of alcohol give positive — CAN does NOT distinguish 1°, 2°, 3° (unlike Lucas)
Trap 2: Ethers give NEGATIVE — the oxygen is not a free -OH
Trap 3: CAN test is specific for the -OH group in alcohols
Trap 4: Phenols give a different colour response — should be tested separately with FeCl₃

fecl3_test.name: FeCl₃ Test (Ferric Chloride Test)
fecl3_test.conditions: Add neutral FeCl₃ solution dropwise; observe immediate colour change
fecl3_test.positive_result: Violet/purple colour (phenols give intense violet); enols give red/violet
fecl3_test.scope_note: Phenols (Ar-OH) and enols (C=C-OH); also compounds with chelatable C=C-OH arrangement

false_positives: Acetylacetone (pentan-2,4-dione) gives intense red-violet — it exists predominantly as enol form (C=C-OH) which chelates Fe³⁺
Salicylic acid gives intense purple (both phenol and carboxylate chelate Fe³⁺)
Hydroxamic acids give red-brown colour
false_negatives: Carboxylic acids give buff/yellow-brown colour (not violet) — do not confuse with phenol positive
Alcohols give no distinctive colour (pale yellow at most)
Amines give no response
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Response varies by phenol substituents: electron-donating groups on phenol → deeper violet; electron-withdrawing groups → lighter colour

fecl3_test.mechanism_note: Fe³⁺ forms a highly coloured coordination complex with the phenolate oxygen; the complex absorbs in the green region, transmitting violet/purple
jee_traps: Trap 1: Carboxylic acids give buff/light yellow — NOT violet; alcohols give NO colour — do not confuse with phenol
Trap 2: Acetylacetone (a beta-diketone) gives positive via its enol form — even though it looks like a ketone, the dominant enol tautomer chelates Fe³⁺
Trap 3: Phenol, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone all give violet but with different shades
Trap 4: FeCl₃ distinguishes phenol from alcohol and from carboxylic acid

ninhydrin.name: Ninhydrin Test
ninhydrin.conditions: Heat with aqueous ninhydrin solution; observe colour after few minutes
ninhydrin.positive_result: Intense purple colour (Ruhemann's purple) for alpha-amino acids and proteins; yellow for proline
ninhydrin.scope_note: Alpha-amino acids (H₂N-CHR-COOH) and proteins containing them; secondary amines give yellow

false_positives: Primary amines (aliphatic) can give purple with ninhydrin — not exclusive to amino acids
Ammonia gives purple colour
false_negatives: Proline (secondary amine) gives YELLOW/ORANGE colour, not purple — this distinguishes proline from other amino acids
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Test is specific for alpha-amino group. N-substituted amino acids (secondary amines) give yellow, not purple.

ninhydrin.mechanism_note: Alpha-amino acid undergoes oxidative decarboxylation with ninhydrin to give CO₂ + aldehyde; the resulting amine intermediate reacts with a second ninhydrin molecule to form Ruhemann's purple chromophore
jee_traps: Trap 1: Proline gives YELLOW (not purple) because it is a secondary amine (cyclic structure) — the purple mechanism requires primary amine
Trap 2: Ninhydrin distinguishes between common alpha-amino acids (purple) and proline/hydroxyproline (yellow)
Trap 3: Proteins give purple because they contain peptide-linked alpha-amino acids (N-terminal free NH₂ reacts)
Trap 4: Simple amines can also give purple — ninhydrin is not exclusive to amino acids

biuret.name: Biuret Test
biuret.conditions: Add dilute CuSO₄ then excess NaOH; observe violet/purple colour
biuret.positive_result: Violet/purple colour due to Cu²⁺ complex with peptide bonds
biuret.scope_note: Compounds with TWO or more peptide bonds (-CO-NH-) — dipeptides and larger proteins; also urea and biuret itself

false_positives: Urea (H₂N-CO-NH₂) gives positive — it has two -NH-CO- type bonds that chelate Cu²⁺
Biuret (H₂N-CO-NH-CO-NH₂) gives positive (namesake compound)
false_negatives: Single amino acids (no peptide bond) give NEGATIVE
Dipeptides give positive (two peptide bonds)
Simple amides (single RCONH₂) may give weak or negative response
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Requires at least 2 peptide bonds (-CO-NH-) in proximity for Cu²⁺ chelation

biuret.mechanism_note: Cu²⁺ forms a violet chelate complex with the nitrogen atoms of peptide bonds in an alkaline solution (similar to biuret compound NH₂-CO-NH-CO-NH₂)
jee_traps: Trap 1: Amino acids alone give NEGATIVE — no peptide bonds; proteins give POSITIVE
Trap 2: Urea gives POSITIVE — it mimics two -NH-CO- units in its structure
Trap 3: Biuret test does NOT detect the presence of amino groups specifically — it detects PEPTIDE BONDS
Trap 4: Ninhydrin detects amino acids; biuret detects peptide bonds/proteins — they test for different things

dnp.name: Brady's Reagent / DNP Test (2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine Test)
dnp.conditions: Add 2,4-DNPH reagent (orange solution); observe precipitate formation
dnp.positive_result: Orange or yellow crystalline precipitate (DNP derivative / phenylhydrazone)
dnp.scope_note: All aldehydes and ketones (any C=O compound)

false_positives: Alpha,beta-unsaturated carbonyls also react (Michael acceptors)
false_negatives: Carboxylic acids do NOT give precipitate (different reaction mechanism)
Esters do NOT give precipitate under mild conditions
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Tests for C=O group (aldehyde OR ketone) — does NOT distinguish between them

dnp.mechanism_note: 2,4-DNPH attacks carbonyl carbon as nucleophile; condensation with loss of water gives a coloured crystalline DNP derivative (hydrazone)
jee_traps: Trap 1: DNP test does NOT distinguish aldehyde from ketone — both give orange/yellow precipitate
Trap 2: To distinguish aldehyde from ketone, use Tollens' or Fehling's (ketone gives negative)
Trap 3: The DNP derivative melting point is used to identify the specific carbonyl compound
Trap 4: Carboxylic acids do NOT give precipitate — C=O in COOH is less electrophilic

benedict.name: Benedict's Test
benedict.conditions: Add Benedict's reagent and heat in boiling water bath for 5 minutes
benedict.positive_result: Orange-red precipitate of Cu₂O (brick-red); colour ranges from green (small amount) to orange to red (large amount of reducing sugar)
benedict.scope_note: Reducing sugars (free aldehyde or reducible keto group in alkaline conditions)

false_positives: Fructose gives POSITIVE despite being a ketose — in alkaline Benedict's conditions, fructose isomerises via enediol to give an aldose; specifically fructose → epimer glucose/mannose in basic conditions
Maltose and lactose give positive (reducing sugars with free anomeric OH)
false_negatives: Sucrose gives NEGATIVE — non-reducing sugar (both anomeric carbons locked in glycosidic bond, no free aldehyde/ketone)
Starch gives NEGATIVE (non-reducing polysaccharide, reducing end is minimal)
Cellulose gives NEGATIVE
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Reducing sugars only. Non-reducing sugars (sucrose, trehalose) must first be hydrolysed to give positive.

benedict.mechanism_note: Reducing sugar reduces Cu²⁺ (complexed with citrate in alkaline solution) to Cu⁺ which precipitates as Cu₂O. Citrate prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation, keeping copper in solution for reaction.
jee_traps: Trap 1: Sucrose gives NEGATIVE with Benedict's but glucose gives POSITIVE — sucrose is non-reducing
Trap 2: Fructose (ketose) gives POSITIVE — alkaline conditions cause enediol rearrangement to aldose form
Trap 3: Starch gives NEGATIVE (only the reducing end can react, but that proportion is negligible)
Trap 4: Benedict's is more sensitive and stable than Fehling's for medical sugar detection

molisch.name: Molisch Test
molisch.conditions: Mix compound with Molisch's reagent; carefully add conc. H₂SO₄ along the side of the tilted tube to create a layer
molisch.positive_result: Purple/violet ring at the interface of the two liquid layers

false_positives: Glycoproteins and glycolipids also give positive (contain carbohydrate moiety)
Any compound that can be dehydrated to furfural or HMF (some aromatic compounds)
exceptions.scope_restrictions: General test for all carbohydrates — does NOT distinguish between monosaccharides, disaccharides, or polysaccharides

molisch.mechanism_note: Conc. H₂SO₄ dehydrates pentoses to furfural and hexoses to hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF); furfural/HMF then condenses with α-naphthol to give purple-coloured complex
jee_traps: Trap 1: Molisch test is GENERAL for all carbohydrates — sucrose gives positive (unlike Benedict's which is negative for sucrose)
Trap 2: The test detects ALL sugars regardless of reducing ability — not specific for reducing sugars
Trap 3: Cellulose and starch also give positive (eventually hydrolysed by H₂SO₄ to glucose)
Trap 4: Molisch's is used first to confirm 'carbohydrate present'; Seliwanoff's or Benedict's are used for further characterisation

seliwanoff.name: Seliwanoff's Test
seliwanoff.conditions: Heat compound with resorcinol in conc. HCl for 2 minutes; observe colour
seliwanoff.positive_result: Ketoses → deep red colour within 2 minutes; Aldoses → very light pink colour after longer time (>5 min)
seliwanoff.scope_note: Distinguishes ketoses (fructose) from aldoses (glucose) — ketoses give rapid red colour

false_positives: Sucrose gives positive (hydrolysed to fructose by HCl, then fructose gives red)
false_negatives: Aldoses (glucose, galactose) give very faint pink after prolonged heating — NOT the deep red of ketoses
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Specific for ketoses (C=O at C-2 or higher); aldoses react very slowly and give only faint colour

seliwanoff.mechanism_note: HCl dehydrates ketoses faster (ketoses → HMF more readily than aldoses → furfural); HMF then condenses with resorcinol to give red chromophore
jee_traps: Trap 1: Fructose → deep red in 2 min; glucose → faint pink after 5+ min — time is the key discriminator
Trap 2: Sucrose gives POSITIVE because acid hydrolysis releases fructose
Trap 3: Seliwanoff distinguishes ketose from aldose (Molisch does not)
Trap 4: Both fructose and glucose give colour, but the intensity and rate are very different

barfoed.name: Barfoed's Test
reagents: Copper acetate (Cu(CH₃COO)₂) in acetic acid (Barfoed's reagent)
barfoed.conditions: Heat with Barfoed's reagent for 5 minutes; observe precipitate
barfoed.positive_result: Orange-red Cu₂O precipitate; monosaccharides react in ~2-5 min; disaccharides react much more slowly (15-20 min) or not at all

false_positives: Disaccharides (e.g., maltose, lactose) do give precipitate after prolonged heating but the rate is much slower
false_negatives: Non-reducing sugars (sucrose) give NEGATIVE even after prolonged heating
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Rate-based test — monosaccharides react quickly, reducing disaccharides react slowly; interpretation is time-dependent

barfoed.mechanism_note: Acidic conditions (acetic acid) prevent non-specific reactions; only the most reducing (monosaccharides) reduce Cu²⁺ fast enough for rapid precipitate formation
jee_traps: Trap 1: Maltose and lactose eventually give precipitate but SLOWLY — quick reaction means monosaccharide
Trap 2: Sucrose gives NO precipitate (non-reducing)
Trap 3: Barfoed's distinguishes monosaccharide from reducing disaccharide by rate, not by colour

xanthoproteic.name: Xanthoproteic Test
xanthoproteic.conditions: Add conc. HNO₃ and warm; observe yellow colour; add NH₃ — observe colour deepening to orange
xanthoproteic.positive_result: Yellow colour on heating with conc. HNO₃; orange colour on addition of NH₃
xanthoproteic.scope_note: Proteins and amino acids containing aromatic rings: phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp)

false_negatives: Proteins without aromatic amino acids (e.g., gelatin-like proteins) give NEGATIVE or weak response
Alanine, glycine, serine and other non-aromatic amino acids give NEGATIVE
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Only detects proteins containing aromatic amino acids (Phe, Tyr, Trp). Glycine, alanine, etc. do NOT give yellow colour.

xanthoproteic.mechanism_note: Nitro group (-NO₂) is introduced into the aromatic ring by electrophilic aromatic nitration; nitro-phenyl compounds are yellow; in alkaline (NH₃) conditions, they form sodium salts which are orange
jee_traps: Trap 1: Positive only for aromatic amino acids — phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan; glycine and alanine give NEGATIVE
Trap 2: Yellow turns orange on addition of NH₃ (alkali) — two distinct colour stages
Trap 3: Skin turns yellow on contact with conc. HNO₃ — xanthoproteic reaction with skin protein
Trap 4: Gelatin (collagen) has very little aromatic amino acid content — may give weak or negative result

millon.name: Millon's Test
reagents: Millon's reagent (Hg(NO₃)₂ in HNO₃, contains Hg²⁺ and Hg⁺)
millon.conditions: Add Millon's reagent and heat; observe colour
millon.positive_result: Red precipitate (or red colour) on heating — indicates presence of phenolic -OH (tyrosine in proteins)
millon.scope_note: Proteins containing tyrosine (phenolic -OH group); also phenols in general

false_positives: Phenols in general give positive (Millon's reagent reacts with any phenolic -OH)
Salicylate gives positive
false_negatives: Proteins lacking tyrosine (e.g., gelatin) give NEGATIVE
Amino acids without phenolic group (glycine, alanine, etc.) give NEGATIVE
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Specific for phenolic -OH group (tyrosine in proteins). Not all proteins give positive — only those containing tyrosine.

millon.mechanism_note: Mercury ions react with the phenol ring of tyrosine (electrophilic substitution onto ring) to form a red mercury-phenol complex on heating
jee_traps: Trap 1: Gelatin gives NEGATIVE (no tyrosine) — Millon's positive does NOT mean 'protein present'; means 'protein with tyrosine present'
Trap 2: Simple phenols also give red colour — the test is for phenolic -OH, not exclusively protein
Trap 3: Distinction from biuret — biuret detects peptide bonds; Millon's detects phenolic OH (tyrosine specifically)

sodium_test.name: Sodium Metal Test (Active Hydrogen Test)
sodium_test.conditions: Add dry compound to dry test tube; add small piece of sodium; observe gas evolution
sodium_test.positive_result: Brisk effervescence of H₂ gas (can be confirmed by burning — gives squeaky pop or burns at mouth of tube)
sodium_test.scope_note: Any compound with an acidic hydrogen (O-H or N-H in amines; C-H in terminal alkynes): alcohols, phenols, carboxylic acids, terminal alkynes (RC≡CH), primary and secondary amines

false_negatives: Ethers give NO H₂ (no active hydrogen)
Tertiary amines give NO H₂ (no N-H bond)
Alkyl halides give NO H₂
Esters give very slow/no reaction at RT
Ketones and aldehydes give no direct H₂ with Na metal at RT (alpha-H is too weakly acidic under mild conditions)
exceptions.scope_restrictions: Requires an acidic enough O-H or N-H. Acidity order: carboxylic acid > phenol > water > terminal alkyne > alcohol > amine (1° > 2° > 3°=0)

sodium_test.mechanism_note: Na reacts with any compound that can donate an H⁺ (Brønsted acid): R-OH + Na → R-ONa + ½H₂; RC≡C-H + Na → RC≡C-Na + ½H₂; R-NH₂ + Na → R-NHNa + ½H₂
jee_traps: Trap 1: Diethyl ether gives NO H₂ with Na — common trap; O in ether cannot donate H⁺ (no O-H)
Trap 2: Tertiary amines (R₃N) give NO H₂ — no N-H bond; primary and secondary amines give H₂
Trap 3: Terminal alkynes (RC≡CH) give H₂ because sp carbon is acidic enough; internal alkynes (RC≡CR') give NO H₂
Trap 4: Carboxylic acids react most vigorously; alcohols react moderately; amines react slowly
Trap 5: Phenol reacts with Na (but more slowly than carboxylic acid) — 2C₆H₅OH + 2Na → 2C₆H₅ONa + H₂
